parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I noticed my chest gets tight whenever I hear a sudden loud noise, which is weird because it never used to bother me at all. I just sit there waiting for the next thing to happen, feeling completely on edge for no real reason. My hands start sweating and I have to consciously remind myself to breathe normally. It feels like my body is stuck in this weird alert mode over absolutely nothing. I try to shake it off, but the tension just lingers in my shoulders all day.
turns.model_response:
It sounds like you're experiencing some really uncomfortable physical sensations and a heightened sense of anxiety in response to sudden loud noises. That feeling of your chest tightening, being on edge, your hands sweating, and the lingering tension in your shoulders can be quite distressing, especially when it feels like there's "no real reason" for it.

It's understandable that you're finding this change concerning, especially since it's something new for you. Our bodies can react in surprising ways, and sometimes these reactions can feel out of proportion to the trigger. The feeling of being "stuck in alert mode" is a very common way to describe the physical symptoms of anxiety.

Have you noticed if this started happening after any particular event or period of stress in your life? Sometimes, even if it doesn't seem directly related, our bodies can hold onto stress and manifest it in new ways.

It's good that you're aware of these changes and how they're affecting you. If these feelings persist or are causing you significant distress, it might be helpful to talk to a doctor or a mental health professional. They can help you understand what might be going on and explore strategies to manage these reactions.

turns.user_message: This constant state of readiness has completely taken over my routine, and I keep wondering why my nerves are so frayed all the time now. I catch myself jumping at the smallest sounds, like a door closing down the hall or a phone vibrating on the table. My stomach knots up before I even know what triggered it, leaving me exhausted from just trying to stay calm. It used to be so easy to brush things off, but now every little shift in the room feels like a warning sign. I am just so tired of feeling like I am waiting for the other shoe to drop.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to be in a constant state of hypervigilance. That feeling of your nerves being frayed all the time, jumping at every little sound, and having your stomach knot up without even knowing why – that's a really tough way to live. It makes complete sense that you're exhausted from the sheer effort of trying to stay calm when your body is constantly on alert.

It's understandable that you miss the ease of brushing things off and that every small shift feels like a warning. That feeling of waiting for the other shoe to drop is a heavy burden to carry. It sounds like your system is working overtime to keep you safe, but it's taking a huge toll on your energy and peace of mind.

turns.user_message: I feel completely trapped inside my own skin because my body refuses to stand down even when everything around me is perfectly quiet. Every single breath feels heavy and shallow, like my lungs are fighting against an invisible weight that just will not lift. I pace back and forth trying to burn off this frantic energy, but my heart keeps hammering against my ribs like a trapped bird. It is exhausting to live like this when I know there is absolutely nothing to be afraid of right now. I just want my nervous system to finally remember how to relax and let me be still.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly difficult to be experiencing such intense physical sensations when there's no apparent external threat. That feeling of being trapped within your own body, with every breath and heartbeat amplifying a sense of unease, must be exhausting and frustrating. It's completely understandable that you're longing for your nervous system to find a sense of calm and stillness.

It takes a lot of strength to articulate these feelings, and I want you to know that you're not alone in experiencing this kind of internal struggle. Many people grapple with their nervous systems feeling overactive even in peaceful environments.

While I can't offer medical advice, if these feelings persist and significantly impact your daily life, it might be helpful to explore them further with a healthcare professional or a therapist. They can offer strategies and support tailored to understanding and managing these kinds of responses.
